name: North Ridge
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: West Route
gear: light alpine rock rack (small-medium cams, few nuts, slings)
pro_needs: Rope and a light alpine rock rack for the roped 5.6-5.8 climbing on the south ridge/summit block; crampons for snow sections.

name: Rampart Ridge Scramble
pro_needs: None required — non-technical Class 2 scramble; no rope or protection needed, though trekking poles help on the loose upper section.

name: West Route
gear: Ice axe (early-season snow in the basin/gullies)
Bear canister (recommended in the Pasayten backcountry)
pro_needs: None required for the standard scramble; a helmet is recommended given loose rock on the upper slopes.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (recommended into early summer for lingering snow)
microspikes or crampons (steep duff/scree and snow patches)
pro_needs: None – standard route is unroped Class 3-4 scrambling; no technical rock or ice protection is placed

name: Beyond Redlining
gear: 60m rope (a 70m is helpful if retreat becomes necessary)

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Class 3 scramble on notably loose rock, worst in the final 500 ft below the summit; a helmet is important, and an ice axe/crampons plus a short rope for less-experienced parties are worthwhile in early season (May-June) when snow still covers the couloirs.

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: Mary Green Glacier (Standard Route)
gear: rope (60m; a lighter/skinnier rope is fine for rappels)
glacier travel kit (pickets, prussiks, crevasse-rescue gear)

name: Southwest Slope via Marmot Pass
gear: Ice axe (recommended into early-mid July while snow lingers near Marmot Pass and on the summit slope)
3+ liters of water — little to none is available above Camp Mystery
Sun protection/layers — trail is exposed above treeline near the pass
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking

name: North Route
gear: trekking poles or water shoes for the river ford

name: Southeast Slopes
gear: ice axe (essential through June on the summit gully)
sturdy boots suited to firm snow if climbing before mid-July

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required. The north face holds separate technical routes (up to IV 5.10+ A1) that are outside the scope of this standard scramble route.

name: Southeast Slopes
gear: trekking poles for the long approach

name: West Face / South Summit (Standard)
gear: cams (helpful for parallel-sided cracks on P1-P2)
glacier kit (axe, crampons or microspikes late-season)
pro_needs: A standard rack of nuts/stoppers plus cams for the parallel-sided cracks on the lower two pitches; a single 50m rope covers the route and the rappels from the northwest-shoulder station.

name: Chockstone Route
gear: Ice axe and crampons if snow/ice lingers in the gully (common into early summer)

name: Classic Route
gear: a handful of slings/runners for natural anchors
a few small-to-mid cams and nuts
pro_needs: Minimal rack — small-to-mid cams/nuts and slings for a short crack plus natural horn/boulder anchors; no bolts on route.

name: Classic Route
gear: helmet (loose rock and rockfall potential in the gully)
short rope or sling useful for the optional/fixed rappel near the summit
ice axe if snow lingers in the gully (common into early summer)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: microspikes (useful for the lingering snow patch ~6,300-6,350 ft)

name: Complete South Buttress
gear: rope (for two half-rope rappels on the lower ridge, plus standard-route gear)

name: South Route
gear: harness and 30m rope for the glacier crossing and optional rappel of the class 4 step
light rack (slings, a few small cams/nuts) for the exposed step
pro_needs: A light rack (a few slings and small cams/nuts) covers the short exposed class 4 step; most parties otherwise scramble the class 3 sections unroped in good conditions and only rope up for the glacier/snowfield crossing.

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Typically scrambled unroped on 3rd-class rock; a rope/light rack is rarely carried but can help on the exposed 4th-class Kimtah–Cosho connecting ridge if linking the two peaks.

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: South Route
gear: alpine rock rack (small rack to 5.8+, some thin/A1 gear for the summit headwall crack)

name: Southwest Route / Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Class 2-3 scrambling on the approach and upper mountain, with brief Class 4 moves on wet or snow-covered slab reported by some parties near the top; no fixed rock protection is placed on the standard summer route. A helmet is worth carrying for the loose, broken terrain and for moat/cornice sections when snow lingers.

name: North Slopes
gear: helmet (loose rock and the short exposed summit step)

name: Southeast Ridge
gear: ice axe (essential into July for the approach snowfield)
light rock rack (a few small-to-mid cams/nuts) and 30-60m rope for the exposed summit crux/optional rappel
bear canister or approved food storage for camp
pro_needs: A light rock rack (a few small-to-mid cams and nuts) plus a 30-60m rope is worth carrying for the short, exposed low-5th-class crux move and optional rappel near the true summit; unnecessary for the rest of the Class 3-4 scramble.

name: Direct Finish
gear: Standard trad rack (small-to-mid stoppers and cams)

name: Dolphin Chimney
gear: standard rack light on small/medium cams — chimney technique matters more than gear here

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Class 2 talus/ridge scramble with occasional Class 3 sidehilling; a helmet is recommended for the loose-rock ridge traverse between Lago and Dot. No rope or technical protection normally needed.

name: E/SE Face
gear: Ice axe (crampons if climbed early season)
Light rock rack for occasional protection
Helmet — looser rock than the other lines

name: Standard Scramble
gear: 50 ft handline (optional, for the Class 3 downclimb)

name: East Face
gear: glacier axe and crampons (or microspikes if the glacier has consolidated to hard ice late season)
pro_needs: A moderate trad rack (stoppers plus cams roughly 0.5–1 inch) covers the crux chimney and ledge systems; bring extra slings for tat at rappel anchors.

name: East Ridge
gear: Single 9mm rope or thin tag line (for the two short rappels near Varden Col)

name: East Ridge
gear: crampons + ice axe for the glacier

name: East Ridge
gear: Small rack or slings for backing up rappel anchors

name: East Ridge Direct
gear: single rack of cams to 2 inches
ice axe and crampons for approach snow

name: East Slope
gear: Glacier rope (30m+), harness, crevasse-rescue kit, pickets
Approach shoes or light boots for the Class 3 rock finish

name: South Route
gear: ice axe (early-season snow in the gullies)

name: Elephant Butte Standard Route (Stetattle/Sourdough Ridge)
gear: Sturdy boots with good ankle support
Topo map, compass, and/or GPS (extensive off-trail route-finding)
pro_needs: No fixed or placed protection is used on this route — it is off-trail hiking/scrambling rather than roped climbing. An ice axe is useful for lingering early-season snow patches on the ridge; some parties carry light rope/webbing for confidence on the class 3 step, though it is not standard practice.

name: Southeast Ridge
gear: ice axe (recommended into June for lingering snow in the summit gullies)
trekking poles for the long approach
sturdy approach shoes with good scree traction
crampons if climbing before the gullies melt out (May-June)

name: Standard Scramble (Esmeralda Basin)
gear: crampons (early season, if the headwall gully is still snow-filled)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (early season, typically through late June/early July)

name: Fire on the Mountain
gear: Extra alpine draws/long slings (double up) — cruxes are well protected but P2 and P5 have runouts

name: Flycatcher Buttress
gear: Gear to #3 Camalot with a single #4
Optional #5 for wider crack sections (per SuperTopo)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (essential into June while the approach gully still holds snow)
helmet (loose, down-sloping rock high on the south face — rockfall is a real hazard)
mountain bike (optional but strongly recommended for the 4-mile gated road to Monte Cristo townsite)
light rope/rack (optional — most parties solo the class 3-4 terrain unroped, but it adds security for less-experienced climbers near the exposed summit notch)
pro_needs: Rope and a light rock rack are optional — most parties solo the Class 3-4 terrain unroped, but the exposed ledges near the summit notch may justify protection for less-experienced climbers.

name: South Route
gear: light alpine rock rack to ~3 in with extra slings (protection in loose rock is often marginal)
ice axe and crampons, especially early season for the Ottohorn–Himmelhorn col approach
sturdy approach footwear suited to brushy, off-trail travel and Class 3 scrambling

name: West Ridge / Saddle Traverse
gear: crampons (early-season snow on the ridge and north-facing gullies)
extra water (the 2021 burn-scar approach has little shade and gets hot)

name: Multi-Summit Scramble
gear: helmet (mandatory — rockfall is common in the gully systems)

rope and full rack mandatory on every established line, not optional as previously listed — a single rack to 2" suffices for the easiest documented route (Southwest Ledges, 5.3), but the classic South Route needs a full trad rack, extra slings/cordage for tat-covered rap anchors, and two 60m ropes
sturdy brush-resistant clothing (extensive slide alder and devil's club bushwhacking low on approach)
headlamp — car-to-car days of 12–15+ hours are common and many parties finish in the dark
pro_needs: Single rack to 2" plus slings suffices for the easiest documented line (Southwest Ledges, 5.3); the classic South Route wants a fuller trad rack, extra webbing/cordage for weathered rap anchors, and two 60m ropes for the six double-rope rappels on descent.

name: Southeast Slopes (Standard Scramble)
gear: ice axe (through early July, north-facing gullies and upper ridge)
helmet (loose rock along the ridge crest)
map & compass or GPS (extensive off-trail ridge navigation above treeline)
bear canister (required for overnight camping within Olympic National Park)

name: East Ridge Scramble
gear: trekking poles for brush and talus
sturdy boots or gaiters (brush and loose talus)

name: South Slopes
gear: map and compass or GPS (route is mostly cross-country and unmarked past Diobsud Lakes)
insect repellent/head net (blackflies and mosquitoes reported in mid-to-late summer)

name: Southwest Slopes / Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Mostly unroped Class 3-4; a short rope and a few small cams/slings are useful for the exposed summit blocks.

name: Southeast Route
gear: crampons (early season, for the approach gully and Barrier crossing)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: helmet (loose rock in the talus/boulder sections)
ice axe (through June; carry crampons too in early season for the approach gully/pass)
map/GPS — the route above the old trapper's cabin is an unmarked, user-path scramble through a boulder field
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: 30m rope (minimum; more useful if rappelling from a higher start in snow/ice)
small rock rack: stoppers + finger-to-hand cams (~0.3-0.75) for the crux
ice axe (early season snow on Sahale Arm / Horseshoe Basin)
pro_needs: A few small cams (~0.3-0.75) and a set of stoppers protect the short 5.2 crux near the summit notch; good small cam placements and solid stopper constrictions have both been reported. No fixed anchors — use a slung horn or chockstone for the rappel/lower.

name: West Route
gear: 60m rope for rappelling the summit block
light rack/slings for the rappel anchor

name: Indian Mountain Scramble
gear: ice axe (early season, on approach slopes)
map + compass/GPS (no trail to summit; route-finding required)
hard-sided bear canister (recommended food storage in this drainage)
gloves (for the cable car crossing of the Chilliwack River)

name: South Peak Scramble
gear: microspikes/light traction (early-season snow and ice on shaded gully sections)

name: South Route
gear: trekking poles (multiple creek crossings on approach)
map/GPS track (cairns are sparse through the boulder fields and it's easy to wander off-route)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (early season, before mid-July snow melts out)
trekking poles for the steep switchbacks
sun protection — most of the trail is exposed with little shade

name: Northeast Slopes / Goat Rocks crest
gear: microspikes or light crampons (recommended into at least mid-July for snow/moat crossings near the col)

name: South Ridge
gear: ice axe (recommended into July while snow lingers in the summit gully)
helmet (loose rock on White Ledges and in the summit gully)

name: Southwest Gully
gear: ice axe (mandatory before August; useful even later for the snow moat beside the summit block)
crampons (early season or if lingering snow is reported)
trekking poles (loose scree on the approach and lower gully)
pro_needs: Most parties scramble unroped; a short rope is reassuring for the class 4 crux and for less-experienced partners.

name: Southwest Slopes / Gully
gear: ice axe (early/mid-season; the Easy Pass Trail's north-facing switchbacks often hold steep snow into July)
trekking poles (for the cross-country sidehill traverse)
pro_needs: Usually scrambled unroped; carry a short rope for the exposed class 3-4 sections if the party wants a belay.

name: Glaciated Scramble (Standard)
gear: crampons or microspikes (microspikes are often adequate on consolidated summer snow; full crampons and rope/crevasse-rescue kit recommended early season)
rope and glacier kit (early season or if crossing the Columbia Glacier)
sturdy approach shoes with good rubber for scrambling on loose 'kitty litter' rock

name: Southwest Slopes
gear: ice axe (snow/ice can linger in the summit gully into August)
helmet (loose talus/rockfall on the final scramble)

name: Labor Pains
gear: Standard trad rack to 3" plus an extra half set (bring roughly one and a half sets total)
Tiny cams and small stoppers/brassies for thin protection on P2-P3

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (early/late season or lingering snow)
trekking poles (useful on talus and the brushy, blowdown-choked approach trail)
sturdy boots for off-trail talus and scree
map/compass or GPS track (route-finding is cross-country above the trail system)
pro_needs: Easy class 2 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: The Zipper (couloir)
pro_needs: Screws and pickets per conditions; rock pro for belays

name: East Route (Main Peak)
gear: map and compass/GPS (route-finding among the five summits is easy to get wrong)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (glacier remnant and permanent snow near the col make this needed in every season, not just early season)
30m rope (optional, for the crux band or a rappel if conditions warrant)

name: Lena Lake to Mt Stone traverse
gear: Ice axe (semi-permanent snow/ice field near St. Peter's Gate)
Sturdy boots for loose moraine and way-trail travel
Map/GPS — much of the route is unmarked way-trail

name: Lewis Creek Route
gear: light rack (optional, for the exposed summit scramble)

name: Liberty Crack (Free)
gear: single rack from purple C3 to #3 Camalot with extra tips-to-fingers pieces (roughly red C3 to .4)
eight long slings plus one triple-length runner for the slab-pitch belay (all belays are fixed)

name: Southeast Slopes Scramble
gear: map & compass or GPS (no maintained trail above Lake Valhalla; easy to get cliffed out)

name: Southeast Approach (Standard)
gear: small rack of nuts and cams (4 small cams)
pro_needs: Pickets and a light rack; much of the rock is too loose to protect well

name: West Route
gear: trekking poles (for the Waptus River ford)
optional 30m rope and light rack for parties uncomfortable soloing the exposed summit notch

name: Southwest Route
gear: alpine rock rack (small cams/nuts to ~2 in)
30-60m rope (for the short rappel off the horn if continuing the traverse)
ice axe / microspikes for early-season snow near Terror Basin

name: South Route
gear: rope and crevasse-rescue gear for the glaciated approach (Cache, Middle Cascade, and/or South Cascade Glaciers)
map/compass or GPS for off-trail, unmarked high-route navigation

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (early season, for lingering snow/gullies)
map and compass or GPS/satellite communicator for cross-country navigation
bear-resistant food storage (Ursack or hang) — the Pasayten lies within the North Cascades Grizzly Bear Recovery Zone
pro_needs: Easy class 2 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: East Slopes
gear: crampons (early season, or for the Richardson/Pilz Glacier variant)
light rope for the short rappel/downclimb step (optional, route-dependent)

name: Luna Glacier
gear: A few snow pickets or flukes for the glacier crux
Light rack of runners/small pro for the rock step and summit block
Rope for rappelling/downclimbing off the summit block

name: Southeast Slopes
gear: microspikes or crampons — snow lingers in the Access Col/Luna Pass gullies well into July
optional 30–35m rope and a few slings/light pro for parties wanting a running belay across the false-to-true-summit traverse (most parties solo this unroped in normal summer conditions)
bear canister (free loaner available at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount)
insect head net — mosquitoes and ground-nesting bees are common in the forested approach

name: Southeast Slopes
gear: ice axe (early-season snow lingers in the gully/upper ridge into July in above-average years)
helmet (rockfall is common in the crux gully)
light rope (~30m) and a few slings/rap gear — many parties use this for the short exposed step near the summit and the existing fixed anchor
sturdy boots with good edging for loose Class 3-4 rock
trekking poles for the loose scree traverse below the ridge crest
insect repellent/head net for black flies (3,000-4,000 ft) and mosquitoes near Holden Lake

name: West Route
gear: helmet (loose rock, short exposed class 3 moves on the ridge crest)

name: Summit Block Scramble (from Stehekin)
gear: crampons (early season, for lingering snowfields on the upper mountain)
sturdy approach shoes/boots with good traction for loose talus and slab

name: South Route
gear: ice axe and traction (early season, snow lingers into June)
helmet (mandatory - loose rock and waterfall chimney)
30m rope recommended for the crux waterfall downclimb/optional rappel

name: West Ridge / Southwest Gully
gear: ice axe (needed for the trail's north side and approach gullies into early-mid July)
crampons (the steep snowfield traverse below Kitling Peak can hold hard snow well into summer)
30m rope or a few slings if you prefer to rappel the crux chockstone in the summit gully (a fixed sling has been reported there)
pro_needs: Generally unroped scramble; a short rope and minimal protection useful for the exposed class 4 step in marginal conditions.

name: Mile High Club
gear: a few alpine draws (optional, reduces rope drag)
60m rope (fully bolted — no trad rack needed)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: rope (30m sufficient for the short technical pitch)
small rack/slings for the chimney (bolts reported but unconfirmed — bring backup protection)
crampons (steep snow to the North Col, early-mid season)
mountain bike (optional, speeds the 4-mile roadbed approach from Barlow Pass)
pro_needs: A few small-to-medium cams and/or slings for the ~30-75 ft chimney (reported as three roped moves: 5.6, 5.2, 5.6); a 30m rope is sufficient for the short technical section. Fixed bolts have been reported in recent years but should not be solely relied upon.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: ice axe (recommended into July for lingering summit snowfields)
crampons or microspikes (trip reports as late as June 30-July 2 found summit-area snow firm enough to require them)
trekking poles (for the Eureka Creek ford)
sturdy boots for miles of talus
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: Lava Glacier Headwall
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets for the steep headwall

name: Lyman Glacier
pro_needs: Glacier kit and pickets for steeper sections

name: Northwest Ridge
pro_needs: Glacier kit and pickets for the steeper snow

name: Wilson Glacier Headwall
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets for the steep headwall

name: Standard Scramble (Switchback / Klahhane Ridge approach)
gear: sturdy, stiff-soled scrambling boots (approach shoes/trail runners are frequently described as inadequate for the summit block)
ice axe (recommended if snow lingers, typically before mid-July)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro on the standard line; the crux is a short exposed Class 3 scramble.

name: Lake Ann Approach Scramble
gear: ice axe (essential through at least early August — snow lingers on approach slopes below the saddle)
helmet (loose rock on the ridge and near Peak 5681)
trekking poles for talus and boulder sections

name: Standard Scramble (Appleton Pass / Boulder Lake approach)
gear: sturdy footwear / approach shoes with good grip for talus and heather
ice axe (recommended by NPS for the trail approach in early season / on residual snow)
bear canister (required for any overnight stay in Olympic Wilderness)
map/compass or GPS — the scramble route above Appleton Pass is an unmarked climbers' path
helmet recommended given loose rock at the step near 5,800 ft
pro_needs: No technical pro; an ice axe helps with any lingering snow near the pass early season.

name: Southwest Slopes
gear: ice axe (useful into July, especially on Easy Pass's north side and the Silent Lakes headwall)
crampons (early/mid-season for the Silent Lakes approach couloir)
trekking poles (cross-country travel through Fisher Creek Basin talus and heather)
pro_needs: Generally an unroped class 3 scramble; rope optional for the exposed summit area.

name: South Slopes
gear: ice axe (early-season snow, can linger into July)
gaiters/brush pants (heavy slide-alder bushwhacking on approach)
map/compass or GPS (unmaintained trail, unmarked scramble route above the pass)

name: Elwha Basin Scramble
gear: map, compass, and GPS track (unmarked, off-trail terrain)
satellite communicator or PLB (no cell coverage)

name: Southwest Route
gear: map/GPS track (route-finding through pathless basins above trail's end)

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: South Ridge / Southeast Slopes
gear: ice axe (required into July most years; useful longer in high-snow years)
crampons (early season, on the snow gully/couloir)
bear canister (required for overnight camping in the Blum Cross-Country Zone)
gaiters and brush-resistant pants (devil's club and slide alder on the lower approach)
map/compass or a GPS track (the climber's path is unmarked and easy to lose)

name: Southeast Route via Hurricane Ridge / Cat Basin
grade: Class 2–3 with steep snow early season
gear: bear canister (required in Sol Duc/Seven Lakes Basin)
map & compass or GPS (route beyond High Divide is unsigned)

name: South Slopes Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: South Side Scramble
gear: ice axe (early season, for the snow gullies below the west ramp)
trekking poles (steep sidehilling and stream crossings)
bug spray (trip reports describe severe insect pressure in late summer)
optional hand line/short rope for less-experienced parties on the exposed Class 3 step near the summit

name: Mount Dana Scramble
gear: crampons (if snow/ice present on the Snow Finger or approach gullies)
map, compass, and GPS for extensive off-trail navigation
sturdy footwear suited to river fords

name: Standard Scramble (Royal Basin)
pro_needs: Largely an unroped scramble; parties may want a rope and minimal gear for protecting or belaying short exposed steps depending on conditions and comfort.

name: Standard Scramble (Marmot Lake / O'Neil Pass approach)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro on the standard line; an ice axe helps on early-season snow slopes.

name: Standard Trail (Upper Trailhead, NFR 24)
gear: sun protection (south/southeast aspect, little shade above treeline)
pro_needs: No technical protection needed; early season an ice axe and the skill to self-arrest are strongly recommended for the snow chute.

name: Southeast Face / Leroy Basin
gear: 30m rope/light rack optional for belaying or hand-lining the exposed class 4-5 summit block

name: South Slopes (Bailey Range)
gear: crampons (early-season snow on the cols and the Catwalk)
bear canister (mandatory in Olympic National Park backcountry)

name: Standard Scramble (Constance Pass / Home Lake)
pro_needs: No technical rock protection required on the standard line; an ice axe is useful for early-season snow in the approach basins.

name: Mongo Ridge (Southwest Buttress)
gear: tag line or twin/double ropes recommended for the numerous gendarme rappels

name: Standard Scramble (First Divide / Marmot Lake approach)
gear: bear canister (NPS-required for overnight food storage)
map/compass or GPS track (travel above First Divide/Home Sweet Home is off-trail)
pro_needs: No technical pro; an ice axe is useful for early-season snow on the approach slopes.

name: Main Peak via North Approach
gear: double ropes (recommended — eases rope drag and rappel descent through vegetated gullies)
bivy gear (many parties are overnight; the full traverse in particular commonly runs 15+ hours)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: Ice axe (early-season snow in the ascent chute, typically lingers through June)
Sturdy boots with aggressive tread for wet rock and timber
Helmet recommended for the loose ascent chute
Bear canister (mandatory for any overnight stay in Olympic Wilderness)

name: Standard Scramble (Flapjack Lakes / Sawtooth Ridge)
gear: Helmet (loose rock/pillow basalt on the ridge and summit gully)
Microspikes or light traction (snow lingers in the approach gullies into June/July)
Ice axe if climbing before mid-July
30m rope + a few slings for the low-5th/4th-class chockstone move near the summit if not comfortable soloing it
Sturdy scrambling boots rather than approach shoes
Bear canister (required for overnight camping at Flapjack Lakes)
GPS/offline map or a recent GPX track — route-finding off the Flapjack Lakes trail is the main crux
3+ liters water capacity per person; the upper mountain is dry
pro_needs: No technical rock pro on the standard line; an ice axe is useful for early-season snow in the approach gullies.

name: Bailey Range Scramble
gear: Crevasse rescue kit (tibloc/microtraxion, pulley, prusiks or slings)

name: Standard Scramble (Deception Creek / Royal Basin)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro required on the standard line; a short rope and a light alpine rack are sometimes carried for the exposed Class 3 summit moves and for belaying less-experienced partners.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: map, compass, and altimeter (unmarked/primitive trail beyond Sundown Pass)
multi-day backpacking/overnight kit (this is not a day trip for most parties)

name: Standard Scramble (Jefferson Creek / Hamma Hamma approach)
pro_needs: No fixed technical rock protection on the standard line, but the short Class 4 step and 45–50° snow gully lead most parties to carry an ice axe and crampons, and some carry a short rope for the crux step.

name: South Ridge / Southeast Ridge
gear: crampons (early-season snow on the upper ridge/cirque)
rope + 1-2 pickets (optional, for early-season snow or a Southwest Rib descent)
sturdy boots for brush and talus
insect repellent (heavy mosquitoes in the Big Beaver valley in July)
pro_needs: No technical rack needed for the standard Class 2-3 ridge line; a rope with one or two snow pickets is worth carrying early season for the south ridge snow, or if planning a Southwest Rib descent through its ~6,900 ft cliff-band snow ramp.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: map, compass, and GPS or satellite communicator (extensive off-trail navigation, no cell coverage)
full multi-day backpacking kit (only reached via a several-day wilderness approach)

name: Curtis Ridge
pro_needs: Ice screws, pickets, and a light rock rack for mixed sections

name: Fuhrer Finger
pro_needs: Glacier travel gear; pickets for the steeper snow

name: Gibraltar Ledges
pro_needs: Pickets; the ledge traverse can require care when icy

name: Kautz Headwall
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets for the steep headwall

name: Mowich Face
pro_needs: Ice screws, pickets; full steep-ice alpine kit

name: Nisqually Icefall
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets; efficient serac/crevasse navigation

name: Ptarmigan Ridge
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets for steep ice/snow; full alpine ice kit

name: Sunset Ridge
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets; full alpine ice kit for the headwall

name: Tahoma Glacier
pro_needs: Full glacier kit; pickets and possibly a second tool for the steep sickle

name: Willis Wall
pro_needs: Full steep alpine ice kit; speed is the primary safety tool

name: South Slopes (Standard)
gear: Ice axe (useful for lingering snow patches into early summer)
Approved hard-sided bear canister (required for overnight stays)
Map, compass, and altimeter — NPS stresses constant position-tracking given the lack of any marked route
Gaiters (brush on the Seattle Creek/Noyes Basin bushwhack approaches)
Helmet recommended on the looser scree/talus sections

name: Hanging Glacier
gear: Ice screws for the steep (up to ~65 degree) ice section
A small rock rack for the 5.6 finish on the east ridge
pro_needs: Ice screws and pickets for steep ice and serac terrain

name: Northeast Ridge
pro_needs: Ice screws, pickets, light rack for the mixed ridge

name: Price Glacier
gear: A small rock rack for the bergschrund/rock variations
pro_needs: Ice screws, pickets, and a light rack for the summit pyramid

name: Standard Scramble (Putvin Trail / Lake of the Angels approach)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro on the standard line; an ice axe is useful for early-season snow in the gullies.

name: Standard Scramble (Marmot Lake / Duckabush approach)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro on the standard line; an ice axe helps on early-season snow slopes.

name: South Route via Lake of the Angels
gear: Ice axe (recommended into July for the St. Peter's Gate saddle and headwall snow)
Helmet (loose rock in the gully/headwall and near the summit block)
Sturdy boots for the steep, root/rock Putvin Trail
Trekking poles for the steep descent

name: Putvin Trail / Lake of the Angels Scramble
pro_needs: Normally an unroped class 3 scramble; a short rope can help protect the summit-block moves or the winter mixed crux.

name: North Face / North Buttress
gear: Glacier travel gear (axe, crampons, thin rope) for the Terror Glacier remnant
Bivy/carry-over gear for a multi-day approach

name: Mount Townsend Trail (standard)
pro_needs: None; this is a hiking/scrambling trail with no technical climbing.

name: The Needles Scramble
pro_needs: Standard route is unroped Class 3-4 scrambling for confident parties; a rope, harness, and a few slings/small rack add security on the short steeper steps for less-experienced scramblers.

name: Standard Scramble (above Lake Cushman)
gear: Helmet (required — loose rock and short rock-face climbing throughout)
Ice axe for early-season snow on the gullies/saddle
Light rope or hand line for the catwalk crux (optional, useful for mixed-ability groups)
3+ liters of water (upper mountain has little shade and can be hot/muggy in summer)
pro_needs: No technical rock pro required on the standard line; early season an ice axe and snow-travel skills are important for the upper gully.

name: Mutchler Peak Scramble
gear: Ice axe (recommended into early summer for north-facing snow)
Sturdy boots with good tread for steep heather and slab
Trekking poles for the brushy approach
Map/compass or GPS — travel is cross-country with no maintained trail
Satellite communicator (InReach/Zoleo) given unreliable cell coverage

name: South Route
gear: Light rope (~30m) and a few slings for the crux notch/downclimb
Rock shoes or sticky-soled approach shoes for the polished slab and summit moves
Bear canister (recommended/required for NPS backcountry camping)
pro_needs: Minimal rack — a light rope plus a few slings/small pieces to back up or replace the crux notch anchor; this is a scramble with a short technical crux, not a pitched rock climb.

name: Neve Glacier / West Ridge
gear: Glacier rope and crevasse-rescue kit (pickets, prusiks)
Sturdy boots for talus and bushwhacking
Blue bags/WAG bags (no toilets in the Colonial/Snowfield zones)
Extra water capacity for a dry camp

name: North Face
gear: light rack of small-to-medium cams and stoppers
a few long slings for horn-anchor rappels on descent

name: Southwest Slopes / South Ridge
gear: Ice axe (early-season snow in the basin/bowl, typically lingers into June-early July)
Sturdy boots with good ankle support for extended scree/talus travel
Trekking poles (creek crossings near Gardner Meadows, loose scree)
Sun protection - hat, sunscreen, sun shirt (minimal shade through the 2021 Cedar Creek Fire burn scar)
Map/compass or GPS (off-trail navigation from Gardner Meadows through Wolf Creek Basin)
Extra water capacity (unreliable water sources in the burned sections and on the scree slopes above Gardner Meadows)

name: North Ridge
gear: Ice axe (snow patches on the ridge and approach persist into midsummer)
Helmet (loose rock in the final approach to the summit)
Light rack optional only if straying onto 4th-class terrain

name: North Ridge
gear: Small-to-mid rock rack (nuts + cams to roughly 2 in) for the crux notch
Helmet — mandatory given the loose rock

name: North Ridge
pro_needs: Standard alpine protection for 5.7 terrain; exact rack size not documented in available trip reports.

name: North Twin Sister Olivine Scramble
gear: Approach shoes with sticky rubber for the olivine scrambling
Ice axe (and possibly crampons) if the line passes near residual snow/glacier terrain on the saddle

name: West Ridge
gear: optional mountain bike for the lower logging-road approach
optional light rope for a hand line near the summit (rarely needed)

name: Northwest Buttress
gear: Medium alpine rack to 3in, including a #4 Camalot for pitch 1

name: NW Ridge
gear: a single 25-30m rope is sufficient for the descent rappel

name: South Ridge / PCT approach
gear: Ice axe (often needed into July for the PCT snowfield below the summit)
Helmet (loose rock/talus on the final scramble)
Microspikes or lightweight crampons for early-season or early-morning hardpack snow
Wind/shell layer — the crest is fully exposed
pro_needs: No technical protection is used on the standard route — it is a trail hike to a Class 2–3 scramble finish. Trekking poles are commonly carried for the snowfields and loose talus.

name: Blue Glacier / Snow Dome — East Face Ramps Finish (Standard)
grade: Grade III; Class 4 with a Class 5 step

name: Mount Olympus Traverse (West–Middle–East Peaks)
grade: Grade III; glacier with Class 4-5 summit work

name: Open Book
gear: a few slings for natural anchors
light rack optional — many parties climb it without placing much gear
pro_needs: Light rack; a rope and a few runners for the natural anchor and belay/rappel are the main requirement — approach shoes are fine, rock shoes not required.

name: West Slopes Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: Southeast Route
gear: Light alpine rock rack (small-to-mid cams and a few nuts) for low-5th to 5.7 pitches
pro_needs: A light alpine rock rack is sufficient for the scattered low-5th-class to 5.7 moves; belays are typically natural gear anchors rather than fixed stations.

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Easy class 2 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: South Route
gear: 3-4 snow pickets or flukes for the pocket glacier and bergschrund crossing
Light rack for the Class 4 sections (a few slings and small cams/nuts)

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Generally an unroped class 3 scramble; rope optional for the exposed summit block.

name: Pinnacle Saddle / Southeast Slopes
gear: Sturdy hiking boots for loose talus and heather
Trekking poles for the switchbacking approach trail
Microspikes or a light ice axe if attempting before mid-July
Sun protection - fully exposed above treeline
Rain shell/layers - afternoon storms build quickly

name: Standard Scramble
gear: Map/compass or GPS track (unofficial, unsigned path above Rainy Lake)
Sturdy boots for talus and brush
Ice axe and traction (microspikes/crampons) for early-season or winter ascents
Northwest Forest Pass, America the Beautiful pass, or $5 day-use pass for trailhead parking
Self-issue wilderness permit (fill out at the trailhead)

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: Standard Route / Southeast Scramble
gear: Ice axe (snow lingers near the summit into midsummer)
Helmet (loose rock on the ridge and gully)
Sturdy boots with ankle support for scree/talus
Trekking poles for the brushy lower bootpath
Map/compass or GPS track — the false-summit ridge is easy to misjudge
Bear-resistant food storage if camping overnight (canisters loanable free at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center)
pro_needs: Mostly Class 3-4 scrambling; a short rope and a few slings are optional for the exposed summit steps.

name: Ragged Edge
gear: A few pitons/beaks may remain in place from the FA (belays were originally marginal, since improved by route cleaning)
Extra long slings for the wandering P4/P5 terrain

name: Standard Scramble
gear: Ice axe and crampons for snow-covered approaches (spring, early summer, and winter ascents)
Trekking poles for off-trail travel in the South Fork Reynolds Creek basin
Skis with skins and ski crampons for winter ski-mountaineering ascents, plus crampons/axe for the final gully
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: West Route (Rock Mountain Trail)
gear: 3+ liters of water (no reliable sources on route)
Sun hat, sunscreen, sun shirt — the lower trail has almost no shade
Sturdy boots with good ankle support (loose rock underfoot)
Layers for wind and exposure on the ridge
Microspikes / light ice axe for early-season snow on north-facing terrain (Rock Lake basin, upper ridge)

name: Ruth-Icy Traverse
gear: 2-3 snow pickets and a crevasse-rescue kit for the Ruth Glacier portion
Climbing helmet (mandatory — active rockfall on Icy)
Harness plus a few slings/rap rings or tat for an optional rappel off Icy's summit gully
30m+ rope is adequate for a single-strand rappel of the ~100 ft gully

name: Ruth Peak Scramble
gear: Ice axe (for lingering snow on the Catwalk and Ruth's slopes into early July)
Bear canister (required in the Seven Lakes Basin/Bailey Range cross-country zone)
Topo map and compass or GPS (essential for off-trail travel)
Trekking poles (talus/scree slopes and stream fords)

name: Southwest Slopes
gear: Helmet — loose rock/scree, especially with other parties above or below
Trekking poles for the scree and talus
Ice axe and crampons if snow lingers in the entry gully/summit ravine (common through June, sometimes into July)
Sturdy boots with good ankle support
Water treatment — last reliable water is Leroy Creek / the 6,100 ft basin
Sun protection — the southwest slope is fully exposed with essentially no shade
Bug repellent for camp in Leroy Basin (mosquitoes common June-July)

name: Standard Scramble
gear: Ice axe — the ridge traverse and upper basin commonly hold snow into early September
Helmet — loose, brushy class 3-4 rock near the summit
30m rope (100-150 ft is reported as sufficient) for the short roped step and rappel below the summit
Rappel anchor material (sling/rap ring) and a rappel device
Sturdy boots suited to brush, talus, and scrambling

name: Southwest Route
gear: Glacier travel kit: harness, crevasse-rescue gear, rope for the team
Ice axe and crampons for the Dome Glacier crossing
1-2 pickets or ice screws for the glacier crossing

name: South Face
gear: standard rack of nuts and cams (a few pieces in the 0.75-1in range plus webbing/cordelette are enough for the crux)

name: South Face
gear: light alpine rack — many placements take nuts
a few medium cams (roughly 0.5-2in) for the upper pitches
crampons can be useful for late-season glacier/moat crossing

name: South Face Center
gear: a few small cams/RPs for thin sections inherited from the original aid line

name: South Ridge
gear: crampons or microspikes for lingering snow/ice in the approach gullies and on Luna Col (persists into July)
optional light rack (a few slings/small cams) and a 30–35m rope for parties wanting a running belay across the false-to-true-summit traverse — not needed by most parties in normal summer conditions

name: South Spur
gear: Ice axe and traction for the Impasse moats and south-face snowfields
Rope and a small rack as backup for the Impasse's 4th-class bypass

name: Southwest Ridge
pro_needs: Medium alpine rock rack with 8-10 runners, a helmet, and one ice axe (per Mountain Project route page).

name: Southwest Scramble
gear: a few slings for backup anchors on horns/trees near the top

name: Soviet Route
gear: small-to-mid cam rack (doubles in finger-to-hand sizes; blue aliens see the most use)
standard glacier/approach gear for the hike to camp

name: West Route
gear: Helmet (loose rock on the ridge and scree ramp)
Sturdy approach shoes or light boots with good edging for the ledges near the summit block
Trekking poles for the scree ramp and cross-country traverse
Ice axe (and crampons in early season, roughly through mid-July) for the Freezer Pass traverse if snow lingers
Optional 30m rope, harness, and a few slings for parties wanting protection on the Class 3/4 exposure near the summit block
Map/compass or GPS — the traverse and cairned sections are easy to lose in poor visibility

name: South Route
pro_needs: No detailed rack has been published. The FA account notes loose rock requiring 'creative anchor construction,' so plan on a standard alpine rock rack with extra small-to-mid cams and slings for improvised gear anchors rather than relying on fixed protection.

name: South Slopes Scramble
gear: Ice axe (needed for the Point 5261 ridge variant; not strictly required for the direct col route)
Snowshoes for winter/spring approach through the basin
Map, compass/GPS — route is entirely off-trail

name: Standard Scramble
gear: rope optional for the summit blocks
pro_needs: No rack needed for a typical ascent — a short hand line or a light 30m rope is enough to protect the one Class 5 step near 6,100 ft.

name: Southwest Face
gear: Two 60m ropes (or one 60m doubled) for the summit rappels
Light rack of small-to-medium cams/nuts plus slings for rap-anchor building
Ice axe (crampons if lingering snow) for the Itswoot Ridge/Cub Lake approach in June-early July
pro_needs: Light gear only — the technical difficulty is short (5.6) and mostly about managing exposure and loose rock rather than sustained protection; a few cams/nuts and sling material for rappel anchors is sufficient.

name: Standard Scramble
gear: Map/compass or GPS track (route above Star Lake is unmaintained/off-trail)
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: South Route
gear: Crampons (early-season snow/ice in the approach gully)
Helmet (loose rock on the face and summit gullies)
Trekking poles for the long approach and stream crossings

name: Standard Scramble
pro_needs: Unroped class 2-3 scramble; no technical gear normally required.

name: The Cave Route
gear: slings (expect sparse to nonexistent placements at the crux)
pro_needs: Marginal to nonexistent protection at the crux highstep; not a route to rely on gear.

name: South Route
pro_needs: A light alpine rack sufficient for scattered low-5th-class rock protection (the terrain is closer to 3rd/4th class scrambling with a short 5.4 crux) plus rappel gear for the descent; exact route-specific rack recommendations were not found for this named line.

name: The Direct North Ridge w/ Gendarme
Sawtooth Range Scramble
gear: Standard trad rack to ~3" plus a few small pins/nuts for thin approach moves
Single 60m rope typical for the tower itself; parties continuing along the ridge often carry two ropes
Extra webbing/cord for rappel anchors — little to no fixed gear on Sawtooth Ridge
pro_needs: Medium trad rack (small to ~3"), a few small pins/nuts, and extra sling/cord material — anchors on Sawtooth Ridge are almost entirely slung horns and blocks rather than bolts or fixed rap rings.

name: Sawtooth Range Scramble
gear: Rack to 3 inches (doubles in common finger/hand sizes useful)
A few small pitons and tat/cord for aging fixed anchors
Two ropes, for a double-rope rappel off the summit notch
Helmet — loose rock increases in frequency near Sawtooth Pass
Full overnight backpacking kit — the approach is a multi-day undertaking, not a day trip
pro_needs: Rack to 3in; both the SE and SW routes are R-rated (runout/sparsely protected), so plan on a mix of small-to-medium cams/nuts plus a few pitons and tat for anchors — this is not a well-protected or bolted route.

name: The Monk - West Cracks - Left Crack
The Monk - West Cracks - Right Crack
Glacier/Scramble Route
gear: 30-40m rope and a few slings for Peak 1's short roped section
Sturdy boots with good edging for scree and rock
Trekking poles for the scree slopes below High Pass
pro_needs: This is a scramble, not a technical rock route — no traditional rack needed. Bring a single 30-40m rope and a few slings/rap rings for the short roped section most parties end up using on Peak 1 (the hardest, westernmost peak); the other peaks are typically climbed unroped by confident, rope-experienced scramblers in dry conditions.

name: The Roof
pro_needs: Small rack (nuts/small-mid cams) plus slings for natural anchors; helmet recommended given loose rock near the top of the block.

name: The West Face
gear: Consider triples of finger/tips-size cams if climbing near your limit
Small wires/stoppers useful on the crux and upper pitches

name: North Peak (Lookout route)
gear: Rope and a small rack (slings, small cams/nuts) for the chimney and rappels
Ice axe and crampons for the glacier approach
Extra webbing for building 2 rappel anchors

name: Tooth and Claw
gear: 70 m lead line and 70 m tag line recommended for both the route and the rap descent

name: South Ridge
gear: Helmet (loose rock/rockfall is a real hazard on the summit pyramid)
30 m rope for rappelling the crux and upper pyramid
Light rack (a few small cams/nuts) for optional protection on the crux
Sturdy brush pants/long sleeves and gaiters for the Bird Creek bushwhack
pro_needs: A short rope and a light rack are advisable for the exposed class 4 / low-5th crux; many parties belay the steepest step.

name: Standard Route / Southwest Side
gear: Helmet — repeatedly called out in trip reports for the loose summit gully
Sturdy boots with good edging/traction for scree, slab, and blocky rock
Trekking poles, useful for the loose scree on the descent
pro_needs: Predominantly Class 3-4 scrambling; a short rope and minimal protection handle the exposed summit moves.

name: Washington Ellinor Traverse (Ridge)
gear: Helmet (loose rock throughout, especially at the crux gendarme downclimb)
Sturdy approach shoes/boots suitable for exposed Class 4/low-5th scrambling
Some parties carry a short hand line or a few slings to protect the loose crux downclimb
3+ liters of water and a headlamp for a long traverse day
pro_needs: No fixed protection on route; a short handline or a few slings are sometimes used by parties to protect the loose crux gendarme downclimb, but the traverse is typically done unroped by experienced parties in dry conditions.

name: Windy Peak Trail (Standard Walk-up)
gear: layers for the exposed, often windy summit ridge
map & compass or GPS (trail junctions among the Long Swamp/Windy Creek/Iron Gate options can be confusing)
insect repellent (mosquitoes are heavy in the burned/regrowth sections early season)
pro_needs: None — this is a non-technical maintained-trail walk-up. No climbing gear required.

name: Wings
gear: Standard trad rack (small-to-mid stoppers and cams)
Helmet — loose rock reported above the first belay

name: With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within
Standard Scramble
gear: Bear canister or hang system for food storage
Water treatment (last reliable water is generally at creek crossings well below the summit)
Green Trails Map #20 (Coleman Peak, WA) and compass/GPS for the off-trail summit slopes
Layers for sudden alpine weather, even in summer
pro_needs: Easy class 2 scramble; no technical gear normally required.
